content:
Is there a general recommendation how to use LockedBy::access_mut() on multiple objects simultaneously, protected by the same lock? access_mut() takes a mutable reference to ensure safe access, but I obviously cannot provide the same reference to multiple calls at the same time.

I have a patch (see below) to reduce the requirement to just providing the mutable reference once, without borrowing it for the entire lifetime (obviously making the interface unsafe). This makes it very flexible to use.

But I would also be open to other suggestions like LockedBy::access_mut_multiple(v: [&'a LockedBy<...>; N], owner: &'a mut U) -> [&'a mut T; N].

Let me know whether you have better ideas! Or whether I missed something.

We could also use field projections from @Benno Lossin to make LockedBy generic over a field underneath the lock. Then split-borrows would be able to avoid sharing a mutable reference. But it does not solve the problem where one has to lock n instances of the same object underneath a single lock.

From a7d06b1e5023b5af01fdbe219c4fce68d1cebd45 Mon Sep 17 00:00:00 2001
From: David Rheinsberg <david@readahead.eu>
Date: Thu, 12 Mar 2026 14:00:03 +0100
Subject: [PATCH] rust/sync: add LockedBy::access_mut_unchecked()

Add a new accessor to `LockedBy`, which allows getting mutable access
without mutably borrowing the owning object.

This is particularly useful when having to lock multiple objects under
different instances of `LockedBy`, but protected by the same lock. In
those cases, the caller needs to retain access to the mutable reference,
so it can continue calling `access_mut()` on the other instances.

It is now up to the caller to ensure the provided reference is held long
enough, and a single `LockedBy` instance is not accessed multiple times.

Signed-off-by: David Rheinsberg <david@readahead.eu>
---
 rust/kernel/sync/locked_by.rs | 29 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++++
 1 file changed, 29 insertions(+)

diff --git a/rust/kernel/sync/locked_by.rs b/rust/kernel/sync/locked_by.rs
index 61f100a45b35..8563412b17ae 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/sync/locked_by.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/sync/locked_by.rs
@@ -166,4 +166,33 @@ pub fn access_mut<'a>(&'a self, owner: &'a mut U) -> &'a mut T {
         // SAFETY: `owner` is evidence that there is only one reference to the owner.
         unsafe { &mut *self.data.get() }
     }
+
+    /// Returns a mutable reference to the protected data when the caller
+    /// provides evidence (via a mutable reference) that the owner is locked.
+    ///
+    /// Unlike [`Self::access_mut()`] this does not require the mutable
+    /// reference to be borrowed for the requested lifetime, and thus multiple
+    /// different [`LockedBy`] objects can be acquired simultaneously.
+    ///
+    /// # Panics
+    ///
+    /// Panics if `owner` is different from the data protected by the lock used
+    /// in [`new`](LockedBy::new).
+    ///
+    /// # Safety
+    ///
+    /// The caller must hold `owner` for `'a` and must not call into this
+    /// function more than once under the same lifetime `'a`.
+    pub fn access_mut_unchecked<'a>(&'a self, owner: &mut U) -> &'a mut T {
+        build_assert!(
+            size_of::<U>() > 0,
+            "`U` cannot be a ZST because `owner` wouldn't be unique"
+        );
+        if !ptr::eq(owner, self.owner) {
+            panic!("mismatched owners");
+        }
+
+        // SAFETY: `owner` is evidence that there is only one reference to the owner.
+        unsafe { &mut *self.data.get() }
+    }
 }
--
2.53.0

I think it would be sad if we used unsafe for this. the access_mut_multiple sounds like a good idea, but you probably want to use a tuple of &'a LockedBy<...> instead of an array to allow different contents between the locks.

I don't think field projection play a role in this, but maybe I misunderstood you.

Benno Lossin said:

I think it would be sad if we used unsafe for this. the access_mut_multiple sounds like a good idea, but you probably want to use a tuple of &'a LockedBy<...> instead of an array to allow different contents between the locks.

I don't think field projection play a role in this, but maybe I misunderstood you.

Think of something like this:

struct PlatformDev {
    inner: Mutex<PlatformInner>,
}

struct PlatformInner {
    sound: Arc<SoundDev>,
    input: Arc<InputDev>,
}

struct SoundDev {
    inner: LockedBy<SoundInner, PlatformInner>,
}

struct InputDev {
    inner: LockedBy<InputInner, PlatformInner>,
}

Imagine you want to lock both PlatformInner.{sound, input}.inner at the same time. You cannot do that, since both would need &mut PlatformInner. But you could instead use field-projections to say either only needs access to a field in PlatformInner, like this:

struct SoundDev {
    inner: LockedBy<SoundInner, PlatformInner, field_of!(PlatformInner, sound)>,
}

struct InputDev {
    inner: LockedBy<InputInner, PlatformInner, field_of!(PlatformInner, input)>,
}

Now you can lock both at the same time. LockedBy can verify that you hold a reference to a field of the correct locked PlatformInner.

Maybe a bit over the top, but I have run into situations like this multiple times.
That layout of data with Mutex<Arc<LockedBy< nesting looks weird, why don't you store SoundDev and InputDev directly inside of PlatformInner?

Benno Lossin said:

That layout of data with Mutex<Arc<LockedBy< nesting looks weird, why don't you store SoundDev and InputDev directly inside of PlatformInner?

Because those devices are passed into the respective kernel subsystems, and those ref-count them. It is kinda the model of ref-counted devices, but they have "userdata" that is owned by a specific context.
if SoundDev & InputDev also have other fields, I could see that (do they have extra fields?)
Yes.
Sorry, should have put some ellipses there.

okay. in that case, I think the following would work better: you store LockedBy<SoundInner, SoundInner> (& the same for InputDev) instead and we add LockedBy::new_of_field that uses field projections to turn &Lock<X, B> into *const U (where X has a field of type U)
we could probably use @Gary Guo's field projection prototype infrastructure for this
so new_of_field would have to be a macro, but otherwise it should work

Right, that would avoid putting the Frt generic on the LockedBy. Much nicer to use.

But yeah, this was just a thought I had. I think for now the access_mut_multiple() should work as well.
Why is this needed? Couldn't you just do Lockedby<SoundInner, Arc<SoundDev>> and call access_mut(&mut platform_inner.sound)?
You're then relying on the borrow checker to ensure split mutable borrows are okay

Gary Guo said:

You're then relying on the borrow checker to ensure split mutable borrows are okay

That's the idea. Thing is, LockedBy requires Lock<U> in new(). So I think @Benno Lossin was suggesting to change the constructor to take Lock<W> with U being a field of W, and guaranteeing that via field projections.

Even though in this case we would not have to make LockedBy generic over the field representing type, what is the recommendation on how to pin an FRT in a type? Assuming Frt: Field<Base = Foo, Type = Bar>, do you then store a simple PhantomData<Frt> in the type? Or PhantomData<*mut Frt> to be invariant over it, but allow ?Sized? You don't technically need to store or access the FRT ever, you just need to pin it over the lifetime of your object.
what do you mean by "pin"? my definition of that word doesn't make sense in that context.

the Field trait has Copy as a super trait, so ?Sized is not an issue. Do note that we're most likely not going to enable the language feature for FRTs in the kernel (at least for some while), since we still have to figure out if we have the correct design upstream.
I do want to experiment with it out-of-tree though, so having a git branch in the Rust-for-Linux/linux repo that allows people to try it

Oh, in the English sense of the word: How to make sure any member function of that type uses the same Frt. So if you create the object with a given Frt, any member function it should use the same. Meaning, you cannot make the functions generic over the Frt, but must "pin" it in the type (or "lock" it, or "include" it, or whatever English term here is more fitting).
Oh, I just copied the bits I need. The language feature is nice, but not necessary to get something similar.

David Rheinsberg said:

Oh, in the English sense of the word: How to make sure any member function of that type uses the same Frt. So if you create the object with a given Frt, any member function it should use the same. Meaning, you cannot make the functions generic over the Frt, but must "pin" it in the type (or "lock" it, or "include" it, or whatever English term here is more fitting).

just add a PhantomData<F> field

Benno Lossin said:

just add a PhantomData<F> field

Doesn't this make the structure covariant over the FRT? Not sure you could actually pull that off, but the safety requirements of Field do not prevent you from designing subtypable FRTs, do they? A PhantomData<*mut F> would avoid that.

Benno Lossin said:

so new_of_field would have to be a macro, but otherwise it should work

If we add a lock view type, we could have fn from_view<L, B: Backend>(owner: View<'_, Lock<L, B>, U>, data: T)?

David Rheinsberg said:

Benno Lossin said:

just add a PhantomData<F> field

Doesn't this make the structure covariant over the FRT? Not sure you could actually pull that off, but the safety requirements of Field do not prevent you from designing subtypable FRTs, do they? A PhantomData<*mut F> would avoid that.

I tested it, and with higher-ranked function pointers I can create 2 conflicting FRTs which have a subtype relationship. So I think PhantomData<*mut F> is required (or some equivalent). I have to note that this is a known issue and documented in https://github.com/rust-lang/rust/issues/56105.
hmm I guess FRTs themselves should be invariant... do you mind putting your example in the rust playground? (play.rust-lang.org)

Benno Lossin said:

hmm I guess FRTs themselves should be invariant... do you mind putting your example in the rust playground? (play.rust-lang.org)

This is based on my own copy of core::field, so I did not convert this fully. But I think this gives an idea of what I did: https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2024&gist=da212537a3b8e2bb638f6c8e0f646d67

I can try to get this to compile with core::field, if it is still unclear. But not before Monday.

The trick really is the subtyping relationship of the types A and B, and using this to create distinc Foo<A> and Foo<B>, and implementing Field for those differently, but retaining the subtype relationship. Note especially the last line, where I coerce a to b, which is what I am concerned about.

core::field::Field cannot be implemented, so not sure if you can observe the same thing there.

But field_of!(T, field) is a subtype of field_of!(U, field) when T is a subtype of U. That seems wrong & I'll change it
It should be contravariant or invariant?
invariant
I feel that it is somewhat similar to fn(T) -> field so contravariant sounds plausible too

Gary Guo said:

I feel that it is somewhat similar to fn(T) -> field so contravariant sounds plausible too

I am pretty sure FieldRepresentingType<T> must be invariant over T. field_of! can stay as it is. It has the former as fixed return type anyway.
